title: Supporting the designation of "Scouting America Day" in celebration of its 115th anniversary.
plain_summary: This resolution supports creating a special day called Scouting America Day to celebrate the organization's 115th anniversary. Scouting America used to be called the Boy Scouts of America. The resolution talks about how scouting teaches young people good values and leadership skills. It also recognizes the millions of kids and adult volunteers who take part in scouting programs around the United States.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H589)
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Resolution Supporting Scouting America Day
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports creating a special day called Scouting America Day to celebrate the one hundred and fifteenth anniversary of when this organization was first created. Scouting America used to be called Boy Scouts of America. A man named William Boyce from Chicago started it on February eighth, nineteen ten, after he learned about scouting while visiting London. On June twenty-first, nineteen ten, thirty-four national representatives met together to make plans for the organization and set up a temporary headquarters in New York. The purpose of Scouting America is to teach young people in America to be self-reliant, have good character, make ethical decisions, and become civic leaders by following the Scout Oath and Law. By nineteen twelve, scouts were in every state. In nineteen twelve, the first Eagle Scout Award was given out, and since then more than two million seven hundred twenty-two thousand young people have earned this award. In nineteen sixteen, Congress gave Scouting America a Federal charter, which is official government recognition. In twenty twenty-four, nearly twenty-nine thousand young men and women earned the rank of Eagle Scout. There are more than one million young people currently in scouting programs who together contributed more than seven million hours of community service in twenty twenty-four, helping to make communities stronger across the country. More than six hundred twenty-eight thousand adult volunteers help lead young people in their communities through religious, civic, and other organizations that are chartered by Scouting America. The men and women involved in scouting often do not seek or receive public thanks for their contribution to our country's future. On February fifth, twenty twenty-five, we celebrate Scouting America Day to recognize the one hundred and fifteenth anniversary of this important American organization. Scouting America works to develop American citizens who are physically, mentally, and emotionally healthy, are very self-reliant with qualities like initiative, courage, and resourcefulness, have personal values based on religious and spiritual beliefs, want to help others and have the skills to do so, understand how American social, economic, and government systems work, take pride in their American heritage and understand our nation's role in the world, have deep respect for the basic rights of all people, and are prepared to participate in and lead American society.
